intro:
The Red Sea (Hebrew Yam Suph, “Sea of Reeds”) is the body of water through which God miraculously delivered Israel from Pharaoh’s army at the exodus — the supreme Old Testament act of redemption. The name Yam Suph is applied in Scripture both to the Gulf of Suez and the Gulf of Aqaba (1 Kings 9:26); the exact site of the crossing is debated, with proposals ranging from the Bitter Lakes and Lake Timsah to the northern arms of the sea.

The crossing is the climax of the exodus narrative. Trapped between the sea and the pursuing Egyptian chariots, the terrified Israelites were told, “Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation of the LORD” (Exodus 14:13-14). Moses stretched out his hand, the LORD drove back the sea by a strong east wind, and Israel passed through on dry ground with the waters a wall on either side (Exodus 14:21-22). When the Egyptians followed, the waters returned and drowned Pharaoh’s host, “not one of them remained” (Exodus 14:27-28). On the far shore Moses and Israel sang the great Song of the Sea: “I will sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously” (Exodus 15:1-2).

The Red Sea deliverance became the defining paradigm of God’s saving power, invoked again and again throughout Scripture. The psalmists rehearse it as the proof of the LORD’s steadfast love — “to him who divided the Red Sea in two… for his steadfast love endures forever” (Psalms 136:13-15; Psalms 106:9-11); the prophets recall it as the pattern of redemption (Isaiah 51:10); and Joshua and Rahab name it as the report that struck fear into Canaan (Joshua 2:10).

The New Testament gathers up its meaning. Hebrews lists the crossing among the triumphs of faith — “By faith the people crossed the Red Sea as on dry land” (Hebrews 11:29); Paul sees in it a type of baptism, the people “baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea” (1 Corinthians 10:1-2); and the redeemed in glory sing “the song of Moses… and the song of the Lamb” (Revelation 15:3). The Red Sea thus stands as the abiding emblem of salvation: God parting the waters to bring his people out of bondage and overthrowing their enemies, foreshadowing the greater deliverance accomplished in Christ.
